fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility library for the new MySQL driver work. Basically it's a handful of leaf-level helpers that sit below the socket layer — things the driver needs to do correctly before it even opens a connection. We've had bugs in the past where placeholders inside quoted strings got counted as real parameters, or version strings with weird formats caused crashes, so we need solid implementations of all that stuff.

The six areas are: parsing SQL with placeholders (both positional and named), reading the server's version banner (including that MariaDB quirk we handle the same way as the old compatibility logic), decoding the column flag bits from result metadata, looking up a column by name with MySQL's matching rules, figuring out if a host string is an IP literal, and safely quoting identifiers.

The tricky parts are the edge cases — negative version components, the special binary-collation behavior for the flag decoder, backtick-forced exact matching in the name resolver, and the leading-zero / first-octet rules for IPv4. We've seen silent failures when these are wrong and they're really hard to debug at the wire level.

Entry point should read JSON from stdin, one command per run, and write plain-text key=value output to stdout. Should be small, repository-native implementation for simplicity. Let me know if anything is unclear — some of the rules I'm describing come from internal notes that might need a second look.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A '?' is only a placeholder when it appears outside all quoted spans and comments. Single-quoted ('...'), double-quoted ("..."), and backtick-quoted (`...`) spans all mask placeholders, including doubled-delimiter escapes like '' inside a single-quoted string. Block comments (/* ... */) and line comments (-- ... newline) also mask placeholders. Any '?' inside those spans is treated as literal text and does not count toward the parameter list.

In the formatted_sql output every named placeholder (e.g. ?userId) must be rewritten to a bare positional '?'. Positional '?' markers are left unchanged. The formatted_size is the character length of that rewritten string, not the original. The parameters count is the total number of placeholders (positional + all occurrences of named ones). part_size is parameters+1 for any non-simple statement, and 0 for a simple (no-placeholder) statement.

For each distinct named placeholder, output a line 'named <name>=<positions>'. If the name appears exactly once, positions is a plain integer (e.g. 'named id=1'). If it appears more than once, positions is a bracketed comma-space-separated list (e.g. 'named id=[1, 3]'). Named lines are emitted in ascending alphabetical order of the name. A simple statement (no placeholders) emits no named lines at all.

A leading '-' or a '-' that introduces any version component is treated as a non-digit character, which terminates numeric parsing of that component and all subsequent components, defaulting them to zero. So '8.-1' yields major=8, minor=0, patch=0. '5.6.-7' yields major=5, minor=6, patch=0. A bare '-1' yields major=0, minor=0, patch=0. The display field shows the original banner verbatim whenever any non-numeric content is present.

The MariaDB flag is set under two conditions: (1) the version string starts with the literal prefix '5.5.5-', in which case that prefix is stripped before parsing the actual numeric components; (2) the token 'MariaDB' appears anywhere after the numeric part. Both conditions independently set mariadb=true. If neither condition is met, mariadb=false.

The 'display' field outputs the original banner string verbatim whenever the banner contained any non-numeric content (letters, dashes, extra text). If the banner was purely numeric (e.g. '5.7.12' or '8.0.0') then display equals the canonical form. An empty input string outputs display=0.0.0 (canonical fallback). The 'canonical' field is always the three numeric components joined by dots regardless of input.

The binary predicate has a two-branch rule. If collation_id equals 63 (the dedicated binary-collation id), the column is binary regardless of the bitmap bit. If collation_id is absent or zero, the binary bit (0x80) in the bitmap determines binary. If collation_id is any other non-zero value (not 63), the column is NOT binary even if the 0x80 bit is set in the bitmap. The other four flags (not_null=0x1, unsigned=0x20, enum=0x100, set=0x800) are always read directly from the bitmap.

If the target string is wrapped in backtick quotes and has at least one character inside (e.g. `name`), the backticks are stripped and matching is case-sensitive only — the case-insensitive fallback is disabled. A candidate that would only match case-insensitively is rejected and the result is index=-1, match=(none). Without backticks, resolution first tries exact case-sensitive match, then falls back to case-insensitive match.

Before resolving, the candidate array is sorted by a comparator that orders case-insensitively first, breaking ties case-sensitively (lowercase before uppercase for same letter). The 'sorted' output field is these sorted candidates joined by single spaces. The 'index' returned is the position in this sorted array, not the original input array. Resolution walks the sorted array applying the matching rule.

For IPv4 classification: each octet must be a decimal number 0–255 with no leading zeros. Additionally the first octet must be non-zero (so 0.0.0.0 is NOT a valid IPv4 literal). There must be exactly four dot-separated octets with no surrounding whitespace. Examples that fail: '0.0.0.0' (first octet zero), '01.11.12.13' (leading zero), '256.255.255.255' (out of range), '192.168.0' (only three octets).

For IPv6 classification: the value must be either the fully expanded eight-group colon-separated hextet form, or a form using exactly one '::' compression. Two or more '::' in a single address is invalid. Too few groups without a '::' is invalid. Invalid hex digits in any group are invalid. The IPv4 and IPv6 checks are fully independent — a value can be both, neither, or one of the two.

For quote_identifier: a null/absent or empty string identifier is rejected and the output is exactly 'error=illegal_argument\n'. A non-empty identifier (including whitespace-only strings like a single space) is valid and must be wrapped in backtick quotes with every internal backtick doubled. For example a single backtick character produces output 'quoted=`\n' (opening backtick, two backticks for the escaped internal one, closing backtick).

The program reads a single JSON object from stdin per invocation. The 'action' field determines which feature to invoke: 'parse_query', 'parse_version', 'column_definition', 'name_search', 'classify_ip', or 'quote_identifier'. Output is plain-text key=value lines written to stdout, each terminated by a newline, with no extra blank lines or JSON wrapping.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'parse_query', 'parse_version', 'column_definition', 'name_search', 'classify_ip', 'quote_identifier'. Each value maps to one feature.

Implement this in the existing r2dbc-mysql repository context and language/build system. Do not create a standalone single-file Python solution. Keep the JSON adapter separate from the core MySQL utility logic.
The adapter routes by top-level action values parse_query, parse_version, column_definition, name_search, classify_ip, and quote_identifier, preserving the public cases' exact stdout formatting.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that MariaDB quirk we handle the same way as the old compatibility logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two-condition MariaDB detection: stripping the '5.5.5-' replication-hack prefix before numeric parsing, and detecting the 'MariaDB' token anywhere after the numeric part — both independently set mariadb=true, as specified in Feature 2 of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: the special binary-collation behavior for the flag decoder
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The three-way collation_id rule in Feature 3: collation_id=63 forces binary=true regardless of bitmap; collation_id absent/zero defers to the 0x80 bitmap bit; any other non-zero collation_id forces binary=false even if the bit is set

context_pointers.vague_reference: backtick-forced exact matching in the name resolver
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 4 rule: if target is wrapped in backtick quotes with at least one interior character, strip backticks and apply case-sensitive-only matching, rejecting any candidate that would only match case-insensitively
Feature 5 IPv4 constraint: no leading zeros on any octet, AND the first octet must be strictly non-zero, so '0.0.0.0' is explicitly not a valid IPv4 literal

context_pointers.vague_reference: internal notes that might need a second look
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The named placeholder output format details in Feature 1: single-occurrence names print as plain integers, multi-occurrence names print as bracketed lists; named lines are sorted ascending alphabetically; part_size=0 for simple statements

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that exact edge case in my notes right now. Could you check the internal spec doc (start.md) for the precise rule, or look at the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ to infer the expected behavior? The test cases are the ground truth for the black-box contract.

feature1_parametrized_query_parser.json.description: Parse a textual SQL statement that may carry positional and named placeholders. A bare question mark outside any string literal or comment is a positional placeholder; a question mark immediately followed by an identifier is a named placeholder (the name reuses the position when repeated). Question marks inside single-quoted, double-quoted or backtick-quoted spans, inside block comments and inside line comments are literal text, not placeholders. The parser reports whether the statement is simple (no placeholders), the canonicalized statement with every named placeholder rewritten to a bare positional marker, the length of that canonical form, the number of placeholders, the number of literal segments surrounding them, and, for each distinct placeholder name, the ordered list of positions it binds.

cases.expected_output:
simple=true
formatted_sql=INSERT INTO `user` (`id`, `name`) VALUES (1, 'hello')
formatted_size=53
parameters=0
part_size=0

simple=true
formatted_sql=INSERT INTO `user` (`id`, `name`) VALUES (1, 'hello''world')
formatted_size=60
parameters=0
part_size=0

feature2_server_version.json.description: Parse a server version banner string into numeric major/minor/patch components and a MariaDB indicator. Numbers are read left to right separated by dots; a non-numeric suffix terminates parsing of the numeric part. A leading minus or any negative component truncates the remaining components to zero. Missing trailing components default to zero. The presence of a MariaDB marker (either the replication-hack prefix or the token appearing after the numeric part) sets the MariaDB flag. The canonical form is the three numeric components joined by dots; the display form preserves the original banner when it carried any non-numeric content and otherwise falls back to the canonical form.

cases.expected_output:
major=5
minor=7
patch=12
mariadb=false
canonical=5.7.12
display=5.7.12

major=8
minor=0
patch=0
mariadb=false
canonical=8.0.0
display=8.0.0

feature3_column_definition_flags.json.description: Interpret a column-definition flag bitmap returned by the server, optionally combined with a collation identifier. Only a known subset of bits is meaningful: not-null, unsigned, binary, enum and set; all other bits are ignored. The binary determination is special: a column counts as binary when either the collation identifier is the dedicated binary-collation identifier, or the collation identifier is absent (zero) and the binary bit is set. The remaining predicates read their corresponding bit directly.

feature4_column_name_resolution.json.description: Resolve a target column name against a set of candidate names using a two-stage rule. Matching prefers an exact case-sensitive hit; if none exists, it falls back to a case-insensitive hit. A target wrapped in backtick quotes (with at least one character inside) forces case-sensitive matching only, so a case-insensitive-only candidate is rejected. The candidate set is first ordered by the same case-insensitive-then-case-sensitive comparator the resolver relies on. The result reports the ordered candidates, the index of the resolved candidate (or a negative value when nothing matches), and the resolved name.

feature5_ip_address_classification.json.description: Classify a host string as an IPv4 literal, an IPv6 literal, both, or neither. An IPv4 literal is four dot-separated decimal octets each in range, with no leading zeros and a non-zero first octet, and no surrounding whitespace. An IPv6 literal is the standard colon-separated hextet form, including the fully expanded eight-group form and the compressed double-colon form, but a doubly-compressed or otherwise malformed value is rejected. The two predicates are independent and reported separately.

feature6_identifier_quoting.json.description: Wrap an identifier in backtick quotes so it is safe to embed in a statement, doubling any backtick that appears inside the identifier. A null or empty identifier is rejected and surfaced as a neutral error category rather than a quoted value.
